title: Domain Knowledge: The Secret Ingredient
concept: Understanding the real-world problem you're modeling is what tells you which raw signals are worth turning into features — domain knowledge guides good feature creation.

options.text: Deriving new, more useful variables from raw data by extracting the signal hidden inside it
Deleting any raw data column that looks complicated
Picking which algorithm a model will use

exercises.explain: Right! But here's the catch: how does anyone know WHICH features are worth creating in the first place?

exercises.prompt: True or false: From a single raw timestamp, you can derive multiple useful features like day_of_week and hour_of_day.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! And knowing that day_of_week matters at all for THIS problem — that takes something more than the timestamp itself.

cards.title: Two people, one patient chart
cards.body: Hand the same patient chart to a doctor and to someone with zero medical training. The doctor immediately zeroes in on fever + cough + fatigue together. The other person might just as easily focus on the patient's shoe size.

cards.title: What is domain knowledge?
cards.body: Domain knowledge is real-world understanding of the problem you're modeling. It's what tells you which raw signals are actually worth turning into features in the first place.

cards.title: The doctor vs. the random guesser
cards.body: A doctor knows fever, cough, and fatigue combined is a meaningful pattern worth capturing as a feature. A random guesser with no medical background might combine shoe size × zip code — technically a new feature, but a meaningless one. Domain knowledge is what separates the two.

cards.title: Debt-to-income ratio
cards.body: Someone with financial domain knowledge, predicting loan default, doesn't just hand over raw income and raw debt separately — they know to combine them into debt-to-income ratio, a feature well known in finance to signal real repayment risk.

cards.title: Automation doesn't replace understanding
cards.body: It's tempting to think bigger datasets or more automated tools remove the need for real-world understanding. In reality, tools can help generate candidate features — but knowing which combinations actually matter for THIS problem still takes someone who understands it.

cards.title: Random Guessing vs. Domain Knowledge
cards.body: Same raw columns, very different feature choices:

right.text:
debt-to-income ratio
fever + cough + fatigue
Grounded in how the problem actually works

exercises.prompt: What is domain knowledge, in the context of feature engineering?

options.text: Real-world understanding of the problem, which guides which features are worth creating
Knowledge of how to write code in a specific programming language
The total number of rows in a raw dataset
A type of algorithm used only for text data

exercises.explain: Exactly! Domain knowledge is understanding of the real-world problem — it's what points you toward the features that actually matter.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Someone with no understanding of a problem is just as likely to invent a meaningful feature as someone with real domain expertise.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! Without real-world understanding, feature combinations are more likely to be arbitrary and meaningless, like shoe_size × zip_code.

exercises.sentence: Combining raw income and raw debt into a debt-to-income ratio is an example of using ___ to create a meaningful feature.
exercises.explain: Right! Knowing that this specific ratio matters for loan risk comes from financial domain knowledge, not chance.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best demonstrates domain knowledge guiding feature creation?

options.text: A doctor combines fever, cough, and fatigue into one feature because that combination is a known symptom pattern
Someone combines a patient's shoe size and zip code into a new feature with no medical reasoning
A dataset is left completely raw and unprepared

exercises.explain: Correct! The doctor's combination is grounded in real medical understanding — that's domain knowledge at work.

exercises.prompt: A team with no background in retail is building a model to predict store sales, and their features are essentially random combinations of raw columns. What does today's lesson suggest they're missing?

options.text: Domain knowledge — real-world understanding of retail that would point them toward meaningful features
More raw data rows, regardless of what the features actually mean
A fancier algorithm, which would fix the random feature choices automatically
Nothing — random feature combinations work just as well as informed ones

exercises.explain: Right! Without domain knowledge, feature choices tend to be arbitrary, like the random guesser combining shoe size and zip code.

exercises.prompt: A hospital's data team (no clinical background) and its doctors both look at the same raw patient records to build a readmission-risk model. Based on today's lesson, who is more likely to propose a genuinely useful feature, and why?

options.text: The doctors — their real-world medical understanding points them toward combinations of symptoms and history that actually signal risk
The data team — understanding the real-world problem is irrelevant to choosing good features
Neither — feature quality has nothing to do with who creates them
It's random which group proposes better features

exercises.explain: Exactly! Domain knowledge — the doctors' real clinical understanding — is what points toward feature combinations grounded in how the problem actually works.

exercises.prompt: A finance team builds debt-to-income ratio as a feature for loan risk, grounded in real lending knowledge. A different team, with no finance background, builds phone_model × favorite_color for the same problem. Which is more likely to help the model, and why?

options.text: debt-to-income ratio — it's grounded in real-world understanding of what actually predicts repayment risk
phone_model × favorite_color — more unusual feature combinations are always more powerful
Both are equally likely to help, since any combination of raw columns counts as a valid feature
Neither — features never affect how well a model performs

exercises.explain: Right! debt-to-income ratio is grounded in real financial understanding of loan risk, while phone_model × favorite_color is an arbitrary combination with no such grounding. Next: you're about to build the actual toolkit for doing this well.
